pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Multiplies exponents when multiplying powers with the same base instead of adding them.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Keep the common base and add the two exponents; exponent multiplication belongs to a power raised to another power.

misconceptions.description: Subtracts exponents in a product as though the operation were division.
misconceptions.error_signature: For x^a*x^b, produces x^(a-b) or x^(b-a).
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Name the operation joining the powers before changing the exponents: multiplication of equal bases calls for addition.

misconceptions.description: Treats a negative exponent as a negative coefficient rather than a reciprocal power.
misconceptions.error_signature: Rewrites x^(-n) as -x^n for positive integer n.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: A negative exponent changes which side of the fraction bar contains the factor; it does not change the sign of the value.

metaphors.description: Sort the visible operation first: equal-base products add exponent counts, quotients remove counts, and nested powers repeat groups. The base stays attached throughout.

error_patterns: same_base_product_multiplies_exponents: x^a*x^b is rewritten as x^(a*b)
same_base_product_subtracts_exponents: x^a*x^b is rewritten as x^(a-b) or x^(b-a)
negative_exponent_changes_sign: x^(-n) is rewritten as -x^n for positive integer n
sources: OpenStax Elementary Algebra 2e, Section 10.2: Use Multiplication Properties of Exponents.
OpenStax College Algebra 2e, Section 1.2: Exponents and Scientific Notation.

lesson_narrative.text: Exponent rules track how many copies of one base are present. First identify the operation that joins the powers; that operation determines what happens to the exponents.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: x to the a times x to the b equals x to the quantity a plus b; x to the a divided by x to the b equals x to the quantity a minus b.
The quantity x to the a raised to b equals x to the a times b; x to the zero equals one; and x to negative n equals one divided by x to the n.

lesson_narrative.text: These statements use integer exponents and require a nonzero base whenever division or a negative exponent is involved. Apply one visible operation at a time, then check that the base and sign were preserved.

remediation.text: Pause before simplifying and circle the operation immediately between or around the powers. Do not change an exponent until that operation has been named.

remediation.expression: product -> add; quotient -> subtract; power of a power -> multiply
remediation.spoken_text: For a product, add exponents; for a quotient, subtract exponents in numerator-minus-denominator order; for a power of a power, multiply exponents.

remediation.text: If the final exponent is negative, rewrite the powered factor as a reciprocal without inserting a minus sign. Then substitute a simple nonzero value, such as x equals 2, to check that the original and simplified forms agree.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Reduces the exponent but omits the original exponent as a multiplier.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Write the original exponent in front before reducing the exponent that remains on x by one.

misconceptions.description: Uses the exponent as a multiplier but leaves the exponent on the variable unchanged.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: After copying the original exponent into the coefficient, subtract one from the exponent still attached to x.

misconceptions.description: Moves a negative exponent toward zero instead of subtracting one.
misconceptions.error_signature: For d/dx x^(-n), produces -n*x^(-n+1) for positive integer n.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Subtracting one from a negative integer makes it one unit more negative.

metaphors.description: The exponent does two jobs: it leaves its current value in front as a multiplier, and the copy still attached to x moves down exactly one integer step.

error_patterns: power_rule_missing_multiplier: d/dx x^n is answered as x^(n-1)
power_rule_unchanged_exponent: d/dx x^n is answered as n*x^n
power_rule_negative_wrong_direction: d/dx x^(-n) is answered with exponent -n+1
sources: OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 3.3: Differentiation Rules.
College Board AP Calculus AB and BC Course and Exam Description, Unit 2: Differentiation.

lesson_narrative.text: The power rule differentiates an integer power with two coordinated changes. The old exponent becomes a coefficient, and the exponent remaining on the variable is one less than before.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: The derivative with respect to x of c times x to the n equals c times n times x to the quantity n minus one.

lesson_narrative.text: The constant coefficient c stays attached and is multiplied by n. The same rule works for zero and negative integer exponents; when n is negative, n minus one is farther below zero.

lesson_narrative.expression: old coefficient -> c*n; old exponent -> n-1
lesson_narrative.spoken_text: The old coefficient changes to c times n, while the old exponent changes to n minus one.

remediation.text: Write two temporary blanks before simplifying: one for the new coefficient and one for the new exponent. Fill both from the original term.

remediation.spoken_text: The new coefficient is c times n, and the new exponent is n minus one.

remediation.text: Check for both common omissions: the original exponent must appear in the coefficient, and the exponent on x must change. For a negative exponent, compute the subtraction on a number line before rewriting the result.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Differentiates only the first term of a sum and copies the remaining terms unchanged.
misconceptions.error_signature: The response contains the derivative of the first term followed by unchanged original terms.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Give every signed additive term its own derivative pass before recombining the results.

misconceptions.description: Changes a subtraction sign into addition while differentiating term by term.
misconceptions.error_signature: A derivative term that should retain a leading minus sign appears positive.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Attach each plus or minus sign to its term before differentiating, and carry that sign into the derivative.

misconceptions.description: Copies an additive constant into the derivative instead of replacing it with zero.
misconceptions.error_signature: The original nonzero additive constant remains in the derivative.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: A fixed number has no change, so its derivative contributes zero.

metaphors.description: Each signed term travels independently through a differentiation station. After every term has been transformed, the results rejoin in their original order.

error_patterns: sum_rule_unchanged_tail: at least one nonconstant term after the first is copied without differentiation
sum_rule_sign_loss: a negative original term produces a positive derivative term
sum_rule_constant_retained: a nonzero additive constant remains in the derivative
sources: OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 3.3: Differentiation Rules.
College Board AP Calculus AB and BC Course and Exam Description, Unit 2: Differentiation.

lesson_narrative.text: Differentiation distributes across addition and subtraction. Treat each signed polynomial term as a separate small problem, then place the derivative terms back in the same order.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: The derivative of f of x plus g of x equals f prime of x plus g prime of x.
The derivative of c times f of x equals c times f prime of x, and the derivative of a constant k equals zero.

lesson_narrative.text: A subtraction sign belongs to the term that follows it, so that sign remains when the term is differentiated. An additive constant disappears because its rate of change is zero.

remediation.text: Rewrite the polynomial as a list of signed terms. Place a derivative mark beside every term before computing any of them.

remediation.spoken_text: A positive a x to the m stays positive and differentiates to a m x to the m minus one; a negative b x to the n stays negative and differentiates to negative b n x to the n minus one; a constant contributes zero.

remediation.text: Before submitting, count the original nonconstant terms and the derivative terms. The counts should match unless like derivative terms were deliberately combined, and no standalone constant should remain.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Differentiates a product by multiplying the two derivatives and omits both hold-one-factor terms.
misconceptions.error_signature: For u*v, produces u'*v' instead of u'*v+u*v'.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Differentiate one factor at a time while holding the other unchanged, then add the two results.

misconceptions.description: Reverses the two terms in the quotient-rule numerator, negating an otherwise correct derivative.
misconceptions.error_signature: For u/v, uses u*v'-v*u' in the numerator.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Begin the numerator with denominator times numerator derivative, then subtract numerator times denominator derivative.

misconceptions.description: Uses the original denominator rather than its square in the quotient-rule result.
misconceptions.error_signature: The quotient-rule numerator is plausible, but the denominator is v rather than v^2.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: After forming the ordered numerator, square the entire original denominator.

metaphors.description: For a product, each factor takes one turn changing while its partner waits. For a quotient, the denominator leads the ordered subtraction and then supports the result twice by being squared.

error_patterns: product_rule_derivatives_multiplied: d/dx(u*v) is answered as u'*v'
quotient_rule_numerator_reversed: d/dx(u/v) uses u*v'-v*u' in the numerator
quotient_rule_denominator_unsquared: d/dx(u/v) uses v instead of v^2 as the denominator
sources: OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 3.3: Differentiation Rules.
College Board AP Calculus AB and BC Course and Exam Description, Unit 2: Differentiation.

lesson_narrative.text: A product changes through two contributions: first the left factor changes while the right factor is held, then the right factor changes while the left factor is held. The contributions are added.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: The derivative of u times v equals u prime times v plus u times v prime.

lesson_narrative.text: For a quotient, keep the numerator order fixed: denominator times the derivative of the numerator, minus numerator times the derivative of the denominator. Then divide by the square of the original denominator.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: The derivative of u divided by v equals the quantity v times u prime minus u times v prime, divided by v squared.

lesson_narrative.caption: Structure of the product and quotient rules
lesson_narrative.spoken_text: For a product, add the left-derivative contribution and the right-derivative contribution. For a quotient, subtract in denominator-times-numerator-derivative order and divide by the denominator squared.

remediation.text: Label the two original functions u and v before differentiating. Write u prime and v prime separately; do not combine them yet.

remediation.spoken_text: For a product, multiply u prime by unchanged v, then add unchanged u times v prime.
For a quotient, form v times u prime minus u times v prime, and divide by v squared.

remediation.text: Use three checks before simplifying: a product result must contain two added contributions; a quotient numerator must start with v times u prime; and the complete original denominator must be squared.
